message.content:

Read these files: /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/camera/README.md (the target), and /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md (project context). If the doc makes claims about code/launch/config, spot-check against the real files under /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/src/.
Topic hint: camera section index.

ACTUAL CODE/CONFIG ON DISK (ground truth as of this audit):
ROS2 package src/guide_mate_explorer/:
  nodes: bfs_explorer.py, glass_guard.py, depth_lidar_fusion.py, combined_node.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  launch: autonomous_mapping.launch.py, depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py, depth_perception.launch.py, explore.launch.py, combined.launch.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  config: nav2_glass.yaml, oakd_mapping.yaml, slam_fused.yaml(UNTRACKED-ish/new)
NEW untracked C++ package src/guide_mate_perception/:
  src/depth_lidar_fusion_node.cpp, CMakeLists.txt, package.xml, launch/depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py
rviz/: launch_rviz.sh, turtlebot468_mapping.rviz (tracked)
Root: README.md, CLAUDE.md, .gitignore (tracked); start.sh (gitignored)
Vendored third-party clone (gitignored, untracked, on-disk only): aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/ (full AWS SDK repo with its own .git, ~20 markdowns)
On-disk credentials (gitignored, never committed): Turtlebot-468.cert.pem, Turtlebot-468.private.key, Turtlebot-468.public.key, Turtlebot-468-Policy, root-CA.crt, c9109663-*AccessToken.txt, connect_device_package.zip

Assess the target doc honestly and critically. importance: 'critical'=needed to operate/understand the robot or repo; 'useful'=helpful reference; 'marginal'=nice-to-have/thin; 'obsolete'=stale/superseded/empty-stub. Many tiny section-index README.md files may be 'marginal' boilerplate. Flag any factual drift from the actual code inventory. Be specific in uniqueValue about what is irreplaceably documented here (real hardware findings, IPs, gotchas) vs. generic restatement.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll audit the camera documentation file. Let me read the target and context files.

content.content:
1	# Camera
2	
3	This section documents the robot's depth/RGB camera (Luxonis **OAK-D-LITE**).
4	
5	## Documents
6	- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](oak-d-camera-test.md)
7	
8	## Quick facts
9	- The OAK-D-LITE provides **both** the RGB "webcam" and the stereo **depth** camera —
10	  there is no separate USB webcam (`/dev/video*` are the Pi's internal codec/ISP nodes).
11	- It is driven through ROS 2 via `depthai_ros_driver` (`ros-humble-depthai`), **not** as a
12	  UVC device. There is no standalone Python `depthai`.
13	- Topics: `/camera/rgb/image_raw` (`bgr8`) and `/camera/stereo/image_raw` (`16UC1`),
14	  both published with **`BEST_EFFORT`** sensor QoS.
15	- **Known issue:** on default settings the camera enters a USB 3 boot/disconnect loop
16	  caused by **power starvation** on the Pi 4. Workaround is to force USB 2
17	  (`-p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH`), which is stable but bandwidth-limited (~80 % frame
18	  drops at 30 fps). Proper fix — independent camera power — is **pending**. See the
19	  document above.
20

content.text: This is a section-index README. Let me check the referenced sibling doc and spot-check the technical claims against CLAUDE.md and the code, since there are some potential discrepancies (model name, topic names, QoS).

content.content:
1	# OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues
2	
3	Bench test of the robot's depth/RGB camera on **robot 468** (`turtlebot-van-468`),
4	Raspberry Pi 4 Model B Rev 1.5, Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble. Tested 2026-06-19.
5	
6	**TL;DR:** Both the RGB ("webcam") and the depth camera **work**, but only after forcing
7	the device to **USB 2.0**. On default settings the camera enters a USB 3 boot/disconnect
8	loop. The root cause is **power**, not the cable or the camera. The USB 2 workaround is
9	stable but **bandwidth-limited**, dropping ~80 % of frames. The proper fix
10	(independent power for the camera) is still **pending**.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## What the camera is
15	
16	The TurtleBot 4's depth camera is a **Luxonis OAK-D-LITE** (MXID `18443010B112591200`),
17	plugged into the Pi over USB. It provides **both** streams:
18	
19	- the **RGB "webcam"** (`/camera/rgb/image_raw`), and
20	- the **stereo depth** camera (`/camera/stereo/image_raw`).
21	
22	There is **no separate USB webcam**. The many `/dev/video*` nodes on the Pi are its
23	internal codec/ISP devices, not a camera — there is no `/dev/video0`. The OAK is **not**
24	a UVC device; it speaks XLink over USB and is driven through ROS 2.
25	
26	| Item | Value |
27	|---|---|
28	| Device | OAK-D-LITE, MXID `18443010B112591200` |
29	| USB ID (bootloader / booted) | `03e7:2485` / `03e7:f63b` |
30	| Driver | `depthai_ros_driver` (`ros-humble-depthai` **2.29.0**) |
31	| Core lib | `/opt/ros/humble/lib/aarch64-linux-gnu/libdepthai-core.so` |
32	| Node / topics | `/camera` → `/camera/rgb/...`, `/camera/stereo/...` |
33	| Python `depthai` | **not** installed (use the ROS driver) |
34	
35	---
36	
37	## How to bring it up (working command)
38	
39	Force USB 2.0 (`HIGH`) speed — see the issues below for why:
40	
41	```bash
42	source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
43	ros2 run depthai_ros_driver camera_node --ros-args \
44	  -p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH
45	```
46	
47	A healthy start logs `USB SPEED: HIGH`, `Device type: OAK-D-LITE`, `Camera ready!`, and
48	**no** `X_LINK_ERROR` lines.
49	
50	### QoS gotcha (important)
51	The image topics publish with **`BEST_EFFORT` (sensor) QoS**. `ros2 topic hz` / `echo`
52	default to **`RELIABLE`**, so they silently receive **nothing** and look broken. Always
53	match QoS:
54	
55	```bash
56	ros2 topic echo /camera/rgb/image_raw    --qos-reliability best_effort --field header
57	ros2 topic echo /camera/stereo/image_raw --qos-reliability best_effort --field header
58	```
59	
60	---
61	
62	## Findings (verified)
63	
64	Both streams deliver frames in USB 2 mode:
65	
66	| Stream | Topic | Format | Notes |
67	|---|---|---|---|
68	| RGB (webcam) | `/camera/rgb/image_raw` | `bgr8`, 1280×720 | ✅ live frames |
69	| Depth (stereo) | `/camera/stereo/image_raw` | `16UC1`, 1280×720 | ✅ live frames |
70	
71	---
72	
73	## Issue 1 — USB 3 boot/disconnect loop  (root cause: POWER)
74	
75	**Symptom.** On default settings the driver reports `USB SPEED: SUPER`, then floods:
76	
77	```
78	Camera diagnostics error: Communication exception ... 'sys_logger_queue' (X_LINK_ERROR)
79	No data on logger queue!
80	```
81	
82	…and **no image frames** ever arrive. The kernel log shows the device cycling:
83	
84	```
85	usb 2-2: new SuperSpeed USB device ... idProduct=f63b   <- boots to USB3
86	usb 2-2: USB disconnect                                  <- drops within seconds
87	usb 1-1.2: new high-speed USB device ... idProduct=2485  <- falls back to bootloader
88	... repeats (22 cycles observed)
89	```
90	
91	**Diagnosis — it is power, not cable/port/camera:**
92	
93	| Evidence | Meaning |
94	|---|---|
95	| `vcgencmd get_throttled` = `0x50000` | Under-voltage **has occurred** (`0x10000`) + throttling **has occurred** (`0x40000`) |
96	| `max_usb_current` not set in `config.txt` | Pi 4 caps USB at **~600 mA shared** across all ports |
97	| OAK `bMaxPower` = 500 mA (USB2), up to 900 mA on USB3 | Peak draw on camera+MyriadX spin-up **exceeds** the Pi's budget |
98	| Link reaches **SuperSpeed every cycle** before dropping | Cable's USB3 data lanes are fine — a bad cable would never reach SS |
99	| Streams are **flawless on USB2** | The camera itself is healthy |
100	
101	Chain of failure: boot to USB3 → cameras + MyriadX current spike → exceeds Pi 4's
102	~600 mA budget → 5 V rail browns out (under-voltage flag) → SuperSpeed link collapses →
103	re-enumerate → loop. The OAK-D-LITE is **not designed to be bus-powered off a Pi 4** at
104	full USB 3 load.
105	
106	- ❌ Wrong camera? No — streams cleanly on USB 2.
107	- ❌ Wrong cable? Not the root — it negotiates SuperSpeed every time (data lanes OK).
108	- ⚠️ Wrong port? It *is* a real USB 3 port; the problem is the **power it can supply**, not data.
109	- ✅ **Root cause: power starvation.**
110	
111	**Workaround applied (this is why the bring-up command forces `HIGH`).** Forcing USB 2.0
112	keeps the device on the stable high-speed link; it draws/runs lighter and stays
113	connected. Result: `USB SPEED: HIGH`, `Camera ready!`, **0** `X_LINK_ERROR`.
114	
115	---
116	
117	## Issue 2 — frame drops from bandwidth limit  (consequence of the USB 2 workaround)
118	
119	The device is configured for **30 fps** on each stream, but USB 2 cannot carry it.
120	Measured over 20 s, both streams running:
121	
122	| Stream | Format | Frame size | **Actual rate** | Data rate |
123	|---|---|---|---|---|
124	| RGB | 1280×720 `bgr8` | 2,700 KB | **5.9 fps** (of 30) | 16.4 MB/s |
125	| Depth | 1280×720 `16UC1` | 1,800 KB | **5.6 fps** (of 30) | 10.4 MB/s |
126	| **Total** | | | | **~27 MB/s (214 Mbit/s)** |
127	
128	**Why frames drop.** The streams are **uncompressed**. Running both at 30 fps needs
129	≈ **135 MB/s** (RGB 81 + depth 54). The USB 2/XLink link on this Pi delivers only
130	**~27 MB/s**, so the OAK discards the oldest frames on-device (bounded by `i_max_q_size`)
131	to keep latency low. The numbers are internally consistent:
132	
133	```
134	delivered / demand = 27 / 135 = 20%   ==   received fps = 6 / 30 = 20%
135	```
136	
137	So **yes, ~80 % of frames are dropped**, and it is purely a transport-bandwidth limit.
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Pending / proper fix
142	
143	The real fix is to **stop powering the camera from the Pi's USB budget**, which removes
144	both issues (stable USB 3 → full 30 fps, no drops):
145	
146	1. **Power the OAK independently** — Luxonis **Y-adapter** (data leg to the Pi, 5 V power
147	   leg to a separate supply) **or** a **powered USB 3 hub** between Pi and camera. *(Not
148	   yet done — need to confirm whether robot 468 shipped with the Y-adapter.)*
149	2. **Proper Pi PSU** — the under-voltage flag suggests the Pi's own 5 V/3 A supply may
150	   also be marginal; use the official 15 W (or better) supply.
151	3. *(Minor / stopgap)* add `max_usb_current=1` to `config.txt` to raise the budget toward
152	   1.2 A — helps but does not replace #1.
153	
154	**Stopgaps that work within USB 2 today** (pick by need):
155	- Match fps to the link: `-p rgb.i_fps:=5 -p stereo.i_fps:=5` → ~100 % of frames, 0 drops, at 5 fps.
156	- On-device compression: `-p rgb.i_low_bandwidth:=true` (H.264/MJPEG on the OAK; RGB 2,700 KB → ~50–100 KB/frame) → much higher RGB fps.
157	- Lower resolution: `-p stereo.i_resolution:=400P` and/or smaller RGB output → fewer bytes/frame.
158	
159	### Not yet verified
160	- [ ] Does robot 468 have the Luxonis Y-adapter? Re-cable with independent power and confirm a stable USB 3 run at 30 fps.
161	- [ ] Measure drop-free fps with `i_low_bandwidth:=true` (and whether the Pi can decode H.264 fast enough).
162	- [ ] Repeat the whole test on **robot 436** (not yet checked).
163	
164	---
165	
166	## Quick reference — diagnostic commands
167	
168	```bash
169	# Is the device present? (03e7 = Luxonis/Movidius)
170	lsusb | grep -i movidius
171	lsusb -t                                  # shows USB2 (480M) vs USB3 (5000M) path
172	
173	# Power health on the Pi (0x...0000 high bits = it HAS happened since boot)
174	vcgencmd get_throttled                    # 0x10000 under-volt, 0x40000 throttle
175	
176	# Watch the USB boot/disconnect loop
177	dmesg | grep -iE 'usb (1-1.2|2-2)|f63b|2485|disconnect'
178	
179	# Bring up + verify (note best_effort QoS)
180	ros2 run depthai_ros_driver camera_node --ros-args -p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH
181	ros2 topic echo /camera/rgb/image_raw    --qos-reliability best_effort --field header
182	ros2 topic echo /camera/stereo/image_raw --qos-reliability best_effort --field header
183	```
184

content.text: I now have enough to assess. Let me note the key drift: the doc uses unnamespaced topics `/camera/rgb/image_raw` and `/camera/stereo/image_raw`, but the actual code and CLAUDE.md use namespaced `oakd/stereo/...` topics (under `/turtlebot468/oakd`). Also worth checking the model name consistency: CLAUDE.md refers generically to "OAK-D", while the doc specifically claims OAK-D-LITE. Let me do a couple of final cross-checks.

content.text:
I have a complete picture now. Key findings:

The camera README is a thin section-index. Its "Quick facts" make one notable factual drift: it lists camera topics as `/camera/rgb/image_raw` and `/camera/stereo/image_raw` (unnamespaced, default driver names), but every piece of actual code (depth_lidar_fusion.py, oakd_mapping.yaml, all launch files) and CLAUDE.md use the **namespaced** topics `oakd/stereo/image_raw` / `oakd/stereo/camera_info` (i.e. `/turtlebot468/oakd/...`). The doc's topics correspond to running the raw `camera_node` bring-up command (without the `-r __ns:=/turtlebot468 -r __node:=oakd` remaps), so they're "as-tested on the bench" but not what the operational pipeline subscribes to. The model name (OAK-D-LITE) is consistent with other docs and the FOV-fallback comment in depth_lidar_fusion.py. The USB2/power gotcha matches CLAUDE.md and config.

The unique value lives almost entirely in the sibling `oak-d-camera-test.md`; this README is a 1KB pointer with a condensed restatement.

input.purpose: Section-index landing page for the camera docs, pointing to the OAK-D-LITE bench-test write-up and giving condensed quick facts.
input.freshness: Mostly matches the code/config inventory (OAK-D-LITE model, USB2 power gotcha, BEST_EFFORT QoS, depthai_ros_driver all confirmed against CLAUDE.md, oakd_mapping.yaml, and depth_lidar_fusion.py). One drift: it lists topics as the unnamespaced default-driver names /camera/rgb/image_raw and /camera/stereo/image_raw, whereas all operational code and CLAUDE.md use the namespaced oakd/stereo/image_raw (i.e. /turtlebot468/oakd/...). The doc's names are what the bare bench bring-up command emits, not what SLAM/Nav2/fusion actually subscribe to.
input.uniqueValue: Almost nothing irreplaceable. Every hardware finding (MXID, USB IDs, power root-cause, frame-drop math, Y-adapter fix) lives in the sibling oak-d-camera-test.md. The only content original to this file is the navigational role plus the (generically restated and partly drifted) quick-facts blurb. The note that /dev/video* are the Pi's internal codec/ISP nodes and there is no standalone Python depthai is the one mildly useful nugget, but it is also stated in the sibling doc.
input.recommendationDetail: Keep as the camera section index (it is referenced from docs/README.md and parallels the other docs/*/README.md indexes), but fix the topic drift: either label the /camera/* topics as the bare bench-test names and add the namespaced operational topics (/turtlebot468/oakd/stereo/image_raw, oakd/stereo/camera_info) that the code actually uses, or replace them with the namespaced forms to match CLAUDE.md and the launch/config files.
input.summary: A ~1KB section-index README for the camera docs. It introduces the Luxonis OAK-D-LITE as the source of both RGB and stereo-depth streams, links to the detailed bench-test doc, and gives quick facts: OAK provides both streams (no separate USB webcam), driven via depthai_ros_driver (not UVC), BEST_EFFORT QoS, and the USB3-boot-loop/power gotcha with the USB2 (i_usb_speed:=HIGH) workaround. Technically accurate except that the example topic names are the unnamespaced driver defaults rather than the namespaced oakd/* topics the actual pipeline uses.
contradictions: Doc lists camera topics as /camera/rgb/image_raw and /camera/stereo/image_raw, but all code (depth_lidar_fusion.py:47-48, oakd_mapping.yaml, depth_perception.launch.py:40-41, depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py:32-33) and CLAUDE.md use namespaced oakd/stereo/image_raw and oakd/stereo/camera_info under /turtlebot468 (the operational bring-up remaps __ns:=/turtlebot468 __node:=oakd).
qualityIssues: Quick facts mostly restate the sibling oak-d-camera-test.md rather than adding index-level value.
Topic names shown are the raw bench-command defaults, which can mislead someone wiring up SLAM/Nav2/fusion (those need the namespaced oakd/* topics).
Directory is owned by root:root (unlike the ubuntu-owned sibling docs dirs) which may cause edit-permission friction.

content.text:
I've completed the audit of `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/camera/README.md`.

Summary of findings:

- **Importance: marginal** — it's a thin (~1KB) section-index README, the standard `docs/*/README.md` boilerplate pattern, with quick facts that mostly restate the sibling `oak-d-camera-test.md`.

- **Recommendation: keep** (it's referenced from `docs/README.md` and parallels the other section indexes) but **fix one factual drift**: the doc lists camera topics as the unnamespaced `/camera/rgb/image_raw` and `/camera/stereo/image_raw`, while every operational file (`depth_lidar_fusion.py:47-48`, `oakd_mapping.yaml`, the launch files) and `CLAUDE.md` use the **namespaced** `oakd/stereo/image_raw` / `oakd/stereo/camera_info` (under `/turtlebot468/oakd`). The doc's names are what the bare bench bring-up command emits, not what SLAM/Nav2/fusion actually subscribe to — misleading for anyone wiring up the pipeline.

- **Verified-correct claims:** OAK-D-LITE model, `depthai_ros_driver`/not-UVC, BEST_EFFORT QoS, and the USB3-boot-loop → USB2 (`i_usb_speed:=HIGH`) power gotcha all match `CLAUDE.md` and the config.

- **Unique value: near zero** — all the irreplaceable hardware findings (MXID `18443010B112591200`, USB IDs `03e7:2485`/`03e7:f63b`, power root-cause, frame-drop math, Y-adapter fix) live in the sibling `oak-d-camera-test.md`, not here.

Minor note: the `docs/camera/` directory is owned by `root:root`, unlike the `ubuntu`-owned sibling doc dirs, which may cause edit-permission friction.
